event_title: Huawei and 68 non-U.S. affiliates added to the Entity List
event_summary: BIS placed Huawei and 68 non-U.S. affiliates on the Entity List, imposing license requirements for items subject to the EAR and signaling a presumption-of-denial posture.
direct_impact: Most exports, reexports, and transfers of items subject to the EAR to Huawei required a BIS license.
indirect_impact: Raises immediate compliance risk and creates a base node for later escalation.
evidence_span: Huawei and sixty-eight non-U.S. affiliates of Huawei raise sufficient concern that prior review of exports, reexports, or transfers will enhance BIS’s ability to prevent activities contrary to U.S. interests.

event_title: Initial Huawei Temporary General License issued
event_summary: BIS created a 90-day temporary general license allowing limited categories of transactions involving the newly listed Huawei entities.
direct_impact: Provides a narrow operational carve-out for legacy networks, existing handsets, cybersecurity research, and standards maintenance.
indirect_impact: Temporarily lowers immediate disruption risk while preserving the main Entity List restriction.
evidence_span: This final rule creates a 90-day temporary general license that partially restores the licensing requirements and policies under the EAR for exports, reexports, and transfers to sixty-nine entities added on May 16, 2019.

event_title: Huawei TGL extended through November 18, 2019
event_summary: BIS extended the Huawei TGL through November 18, 2019 while revising and clarifying authorized transactions.
direct_impact: Keeps limited operational continuity for already-authorized legacy support activities.
indirect_impact: Signals that the carve-out is temporary and subject to continued review.
evidence_span: The U.S. Government has decided to extend the temporary general license through November 18, 2019.

event_title: Huawei TGL extended through February 16, 2020
event_summary: BIS again extended the Huawei TGL, this time through February 16, 2020.
direct_impact: Preserves limited continuity for existing network and handset support activities.
indirect_impact: Extends but does not normalize Huawei transaction risk.
evidence_span: The U.S. Government has decided to extend through February 16, 2020, the temporary general license to Huawei and one hundred and fourteen of its non-U.S. affiliates.

event_title: Huawei TGL extended through April 1, 2020
event_summary: BIS extended the Huawei TGL through April 1, 2020.
direct_impact: Maintains temporary support channels for legacy operations.
indirect_impact: Keeps firms in a short-cycle renewal environment.
evidence_span: The U.S. Government has decided to extend through April 1, 2020, the temporary general license to Huawei and one hundred and fourteen of its non-U.S. affiliates.

event_title: Huawei TGL extended through May 15, 2020
event_summary: BIS extended the Huawei TGL through May 15, 2020.
direct_impact: Extends temporary support for existing networks and mobile services.
indirect_impact: Leaves counterparties exposed to a still-uncertain policy horizon.
evidence_span: The U.S. Government has decided to extend through May 15, 2020, the temporary general license to Huawei and one hundred and fourteen of its non-U.S. affiliates.

event_title: Huawei TGL extended through August 13, 2020
event_summary: BIS extended the Huawei TGL through August 13, 2020 and signaled that covered activities might later be revised or removed.
direct_impact: Extends limited continuity one last time for legacy support channels.
indirect_impact: Creates a visible transition point before later TGL removal.
evidence_span: This final rule revises the temporary general license to remove the expiration date of May 15, 2020, and substitutes the date of August 13, 2020.

event_title: Huawei-targeted FDP rule introduced
event_summary: BIS amended General Prohibition Three to capture certain foreign-produced items made with U.S. technology or software when destined to Huawei or other footnote 1 entities.
direct_impact: Extends U.S. jurisdictional reach to certain foreign-produced semiconductor-related items for Huawei transactions.
indirect_impact: Raises non-U.S. foundry and supplier exposure in Huawei-related transactions.
evidence_span: This rule amends General Prohibition Three by adding a new control over certain foreign-produced items when there is knowledge that such items are destined to a designated entity with a footnote 1 designation on the Entity List.

event_title: Thirty-eight additional Huawei affiliates added to the Entity List
event_summary: BIS added 38 more non-U.S. Huawei affiliates to the Entity List and revised several existing Huawei entries.
direct_impact: Expands the set of Huawei-linked parties subject to licensing requirements.
indirect_impact: Widens compliance screening and affiliate-level exposure for counterparties.
evidence_span: First, BIS is adding thirty-eight additional non-U.S. affiliates of Huawei to the Entity List because they also pose a significant risk of involvement in activities contrary to U.S. interests.

event_title: Huawei TGL removed and replaced by a more limited authorization
event_summary: BIS terminated the broad Huawei TGL and replaced it with a narrower permanent authorization.
direct_impact: Removes the main broad temporary carve-out that had supported limited legacy transactions.
indirect_impact: Sharpens operational disruption risk for firms that had relied on the TGL.
evidence_span: Second, this rule removes a temporary general license for Huawei and its non-U.S. affiliates and replaces those provisions with a more limited permanent authorization.

event_title: Huawei FDP rule further expanded to close loopholes
event_summary: BIS further amended the Huawei FDP framework to capture more foreign-produced items tied to Huawei, especially where Huawei is involved as a party to the transaction.
direct_impact: Broadens the kinds of foreign-made items that require a license when Huawei is involved.
indirect_impact: Increases exposure for foundries, component vendors, and indirect suppliers.
evidence_span: Third, this final rule amends General Prohibition Three, also known as the foreign-produced direct product rule, to further restrict items subject to the EAR involving Huawei.

event_title: FCC Covered List includes Huawei equipment and services
event_summary: The FCC’s initial Covered List formally identified Huawei equipment and services as posing an unacceptable national-security risk under the Secure Networks Act process.
direct_impact: Moves Huawei risk from export controls into U.S. communications market access and network replacement policy.
indirect_impact: Creates a domestic U.S. restriction anchor later used by the FCC equipment authorization ban.
evidence_span: Telecommunications equipment produced or provided by Huawei Technologies Company ... Date of inclusion on Covered List: March 12, 2021.

event_title: FCC blocks new authorization of covered Huawei equipment
event_summary: The FCC adopted rules prohibiting new equipment authorization for covered equipment, including Huawei products on the Covered List.
direct_impact: Prevents new covered Huawei equipment from obtaining FCC authorization for U.S. marketing or import paths.
indirect_impact: Deepens domestic U.S. market-access restrictions beyond the original Covered List.
evidence_span: The new rules prohibit the authorization of equipment through the FCC’s Certification process ... The Covered List currently includes communications equipment produced by Huawei Technologies.

event_title: BIS penalizes Seagate for shipments to Huawei under the FDP rule
event_summary: BIS imposed a $300 million civil penalty on Seagate for hard-disk-drive shipments to Huawei in violation of the Huawei FDP rule.
direct_impact: Shows that Huawei-related FDP obligations can result in major downstream enforcement against third-party suppliers.
indirect_impact: Transforms abstract control risk into realized enforcement exposure.
evidence_span: BIS imposed a $300 million civil penalty against Seagate ... to resolve alleged violations of U.S. export controls related to selling hard disk drives to Huawei in violation of the foreign direct product rule.

event_title: SMIC and related affiliates added to the Entity List
event_summary: BIS added SMIC and related affiliates to the Entity List, with a presumption of denial for items uniquely required to produce chips at 10nm and below.
direct_impact: Limits SMIC’s ability to acquire U.S.-regulated technology and imposes a particularly severe review policy for advanced-node production items.
indirect_impact: Creates a firm-specific anchor for later ecosystem-wide semiconductor controls.
evidence_span: The Entity List designation limits SMIC's ability to acquire certain U.S. technology ... Items uniquely required to produce semiconductors at advanced technology nodes 10 nanometers or below will be subject to a presumption of denial.

event_title: October 2022 advanced-computing and semiconductor-manufacturing controls issued
event_summary: BIS introduced a broad control package targeting advanced computing items, semiconductor manufacturing items, and related supercomputer and semiconductor end uses for China.
direct_impact: Pushes controls beyond named firms toward technology classes, end uses, and fabrication capabilities.
indirect_impact: Amplifies fabrication and supply-chain risk for PRC advanced-node actors including SMIC-relevant ecosystems.
evidence_span: This rule expands the scope of licensing requirements for 28 existing entities on the Entity List located in the PRC and imposes additional export controls on advanced computing and semiconductor manufacturing items.

event_title: December 2022 Entity List action broadens pressure on PRC chip ecosystem
event_summary: BIS added 36 entities to the Entity List and made conforming removals from the UVL, extending pressure across additional PRC chip-sector actors.
direct_impact: Adds more PRC semiconductor ecosystem actors to a stricter licensing environment.
indirect_impact: Signals that compliance risk is spreading beyond one firm to a broader network of counterparties.
evidence_span: The Department of Commerce is amending the EAR by adding thirty-six entities to the Entity List ... This rule also revises three entries on the Entity List under the destination of China.

event_title: 2023 semiconductor-manufacturing-items rule updates sector controls
event_summary: BIS updated semiconductor-manufacturing-item controls as part of the October 2023 package, refining restrictions relevant to PRC fab capacity and equipment access.
direct_impact: Refines the control perimeter for semiconductor manufacturing items and supporting equipment.
indirect_impact: Raises process-specific compliance complexity for fabs and suppliers.
evidence_span: BIS updates the SME controls through publication of this SME IFR ... Together, these IFRs advance the U.S. national security objectives identified above.

event_title: 2023 advanced-computing update rule revises October 2022 framework
event_summary: BIS updated and corrected the October 2022 advanced-computing rule package, affecting advanced chip and supercomputer controls relevant to PRC fabs and their customers.
direct_impact: Refreshes thresholds, scope, and clarifications for advanced-computing controls tied to China.
indirect_impact: Extends firm-level risk into customer, design, and compute ecosystem interactions.
evidence_span: On October 17, 2023, BIS released two interim final rules ... including AC/S IFR ... updating the controls released on October 7, 2022.

event_title: April 2024 rule adds corrections and clarifications to the 2023 package
event_summary: BIS issued corrections and clarifications to the October 2023 control package, affecting interpretation and compliance boundaries.
direct_impact: Changes how firms operationalize control boundaries and compliance interpretation.
indirect_impact: Maintains uncertainty and transaction-screening burden even without a new headline restriction category.
evidence_span: This rule corrects inadvertent errors contained in these rules and makes additional clarifications.

event_title: December 2024 140-entity action tightens semiconductor ecosystem controls
event_summary: BIS added 140 entities to the Entity List and modified multiple entries, deepening restrictions across the semiconductor ecosystem.
direct_impact: Expands entity-level restrictions across a broad semiconductor network and raises partner-screening risk.
indirect_impact: Represents another step from single-firm controls toward ecosystem-wide denial and screening pressure.
evidence_span: In this final rule, BIS amends the EAR by adding 140 entities to the Entity List.

event_title: October 2022 BIS advanced-computing and semiconductor-manufacturing rule issued
event_summary: BIS issued the October 2022 control package targeting advanced computing items, semiconductor manufacturing items, supercomputer end uses, and related Entity List modifications.
direct_impact: Introduces a new control architecture organized around advanced chips, supercomputing, and fabrication capability.
indirect_impact: Creates the central policy anchor for subsequent update, correction, and escalation events.
evidence_span: Implementation of Additional Export Controls: Certain Advanced Computing and Semiconductor Manufacturing Items; Supercomputer and Semiconductor End Use; Entity List Modification.

event_title: Thirty-one PRC entities added to the UVL
event_summary: BIS added 31 PRC entities to the Unverified List because end-use checks could not be completed satisfactorily.
direct_impact: Introduces additional red-flag and due-diligence burdens short of full Entity List restrictions.
indirect_impact: Creates an intermediate escalation step between ordinary trade and Entity List controls.
evidence_span: The Bureau of Industry and Security is amending the EAR by adding 31 persons to the Unverified List ... because an end-use check could not be completed satisfactorily.

event_title: December 2022 rule escalates part of the ecosystem from UVL concerns to Entity List restrictions
event_summary: BIS added 36 entities to the Entity List and removed related entries from the UVL, escalating compliance exposure from verification problems to formal denial-risk screening.
direct_impact: Converts part of the ecosystem from enhanced diligence to strict licensing requirements.
indirect_impact: Demonstrates a policy path from unresolved verification concerns to formal entity-level restriction.
evidence_span: This rule also revises three entries on the Entity List under the destination of China and makes conforming removal from the Unverified List.

event_title: October 2023 SME interim final rule updates manufacturing-item controls
event_summary: BIS updated the October 2022 controls specifically for semiconductor manufacturing items.
direct_impact: Refines restrictions on equipment and manufacturing items used in advanced chip production.
indirect_impact: Sharpens technology-access and process-equipment risk across the ecosystem.
evidence_span: BIS updates the SME controls through publication of this SME IFR while publishing elsewhere in this issue a separate IFR.

event_title: October 2023 AC/S interim final rule updates advanced-computing controls
event_summary: BIS updated the October 2022 advanced-computing package for chips, supercomputer end use, and related control definitions and corrections.
direct_impact: Updates thresholds, definitions, and controls for advanced-computing items and related end uses.
indirect_impact: Serves as the main update branch later clarified again in April 2024.
evidence_span: This IFR was published in the Federal Register on October 13, 2022 ... BIS imposed these new controls to protect U.S. national security and is updating them here.

event_title: April 2024 rule corrects and clarifies the October 2023 package
event_summary: BIS issued a further rule to correct inadvertent errors and add clarifications to the October 2023 updates.
direct_impact: Changes how firms interpret and implement October 2023 controls in practice.
indirect_impact: Shows that operational compliance risk persists even in “correction” rounds.
evidence_span: This rule corrects inadvertent errors contained in these rules and makes additional clarifications.

event_title: FAR Council begins rulemaking on covered semiconductor products and services
event_summary: The FAR Council began rulemaking to implement Section 5949 restrictions on covered semiconductor products and services in federal procurement.
direct_impact: Adds a procurement and contractor-screening dimension to semiconductor security controls.
indirect_impact: Extends control logic from export licensing into federal acquisition and supply-chain governance.
evidence_span: Require contractors to conduct a reasonable inquiry to detect and avoid the use or inclusion of covered semiconductor products or services in electronic products and electronic services.

event_title: December 2024 BIS 140-entity action deepens ecosystem-wide restrictions
event_summary: BIS added 140 entities to the Entity List and modified multiple entries, tightening controls across the semiconductor and advanced-computing ecosystem.
direct_impact: Greatly increases screening, licensing, and transaction-risk burdens across the ecosystem.
indirect_impact: Represents a large-scale ecosystem tightening rather than isolated case-by-case action.
evidence_span: In this final rule, BIS amends the EAR by adding 140 entities to the Entity List.

event_title: December 2024 companion IFR adds HBM controls and new FDP refinements
event_summary: BIS paired the December 2024 Entity List expansion with a companion IFR that added high-bandwidth-memory controls, revised advanced-node DRAM criteria, and created new FDP rules tied to advanced-node IC production.
target_entity: PRC advanced-node IC and HBM ecosystem
direct_impact: Deepens technical controls beyond entity listings by targeting HBM, advanced-node DRAM definitions, and foreign-produced items tied to advanced-node IC production.
indirect_impact: Raises both technology-access and fabrication-compliance risk for PRC advanced-computing and memory-fab ecosystems.
evidence_span: BIS states that the IFR adds HBM controls, creates new FDP rules for advanced-node IC production, and refines controls on advanced computing and semiconductor manufacturing items.

event_title: YMTC added to the Unverified List
event_summary: BIS added YMTC to the Unverified List because an end-use check could not be completed satisfactorily, making YMTC part of the October 2022 verification-risk tightening against 31 PRC persons.
direct_impact: Creates unresolved end-use verification risk and elevates screening friction for transactions involving YMTC.
indirect_impact: Creates a pathway for later escalation if the verification problem is not resolved.
evidence_span: BIS added 31 PRC persons to the UVL because end-use checks could not be completed satisfactorily; the list explicitly included Yangtze Memory Technologies Co., Ltd.

event_title: YMTC moved from UVL pressure to the Entity List
event_summary: BIS added YMTC to the Entity List, citing significant national security and foreign-policy concerns, and removed YMTC from the UVL for consistency.
direct_impact: Transforms verification risk into formal Entity List restrictions with a presumption-of-denial posture.
indirect_impact: Hardens downstream supply, tooling, and compliance risk for YMTC-linked chains.
evidence_span: The December 2022 rule added Yangtze Memory Technologies Co., Ltd. to the Entity List and removed it from the UVL for consistency.

event_title: December 2024 BIS refinements tighten HBM and advanced-node memory controls
event_summary: BIS added HBM controls and revised advanced-node DRAM criteria in December 2024 to prevent workarounds and reduce the risk that U.S. technology contributes to PRC advanced-node IC production.
direct_impact: Tightens controls on advanced-memory-relevant items, including HBM and advanced-node DRAM-related definitions.
indirect_impact: Increases the likelihood that PRC memory-fab actors face narrowed access and higher compliance burdens even without a new entity-specific listing.
evidence_span: The December 2024 IFR added HBM controls and revised advanced-node DRAM criteria to prevent future workarounds and reduce the risk that U.S. technology contributes to PRC advanced-node IC production.

event_title: Proposed FAR rule names YMTC and CXMT as covered semiconductor sources
event_summary: The FAR Council proposed a rule that defines covered semiconductor products or services to include those designed, produced, or provided by CXMT or YMTC, extending the consequences of tech-security restrictions into federal procurement and supply-chain disclosure.
actor: FAR Council / OFPP / DoD / GSA / NASA
direct_impact: Introduces federal procurement-exclusion risk for products or services tied to YMTC and CXMT ahead of the December 2027 effective date.
indirect_impact: Broadens the consequence space from export licensing into procurement screening, disclosure, and remediation obligations.
evidence_span: The February 2026 proposed FAR rule defines covered semiconductor products or services to include those designed, produced, or provided by ChangXin Memory Technologies or Yangtze Memory Technologies Corp.

event_title: FAR Council begins covered-semiconductor rulemaking
event_summary: DoD, GSA, and NASA issued an ANPR to implement Section 5949, beginning formal rulemaking on procurement restrictions for electronic products and services that include covered semiconductor products or services effective December 23, 2027.
direct_impact: Signals that covered semiconductors will become a federal procurement and sourcing problem, not just an export-control issue.
indirect_impact: Pushes contractors and suppliers to assess future exposure before a final procurement regime is in place.
evidence_span: The May 2024 ANPR states that Section 5949 prohibits executive agencies from procuring or obtaining certain products and services that include covered semiconductor products or services effective December 23, 2027.

event_title: Covered-semiconductor ANPR comment period extended
event_summary: The FAR Council extended the comment period for the covered-semiconductor ANPR to August 2, 2024, giving industry additional time to respond and signaling continuing rule development.
direct_impact: Extends the consultation window and confirms that implementation questions remain active in rulemaking.
indirect_impact: Keeps procurement-risk planning live for contractors and semiconductor supply-chain participants.
evidence_span: The June 2024 notice extended the comment period for the May 2024 ANPR from July 2, 2024 to August 2, 2024.

event_title: FAR Council proposes covered-semiconductor procurement prohibition
event_summary: The FAR Council proposed amendments to the FAR to implement Section 5949, including a procurement prohibition effective December 23, 2027 for covered semiconductor products or services, absent an exception or waiver.
actor: FAR Council / OFPP / DoD / GSA / NASA
direct_impact: Turns the earlier ANPR into a concrete proposed procurement prohibition with operative contract language and timelines.
indirect_impact: Creates direct federal market-access risk for covered semiconductor products and downstream systems using them in critical systems.
evidence_span: The February 2026 proposed rule would prohibit agencies, on or after December 23, 2027, from procuring or obtaining electronic products or services that include covered semiconductor products or services.

event_title: Proposed FAR rule explicitly names SMIC, CXMT, and YMTC in covered-source definition
event_summary: The proposed rule operationalizes the prohibition by defining covered semiconductor products or services to include those designed, produced, or provided by SMIC, CXMT, or YMTC and their subsidiaries, affiliates, or successors.
actor: FAR Council / OFPP / DoD / GSA / NASA
direct_impact: Sharpens vendor-screening obligations by tying the rule to specific named Chinese semiconductor producers.
indirect_impact: Amplifies supplier-traceability and sourcing risk for contractors that rely on products linked to those entities.
evidence_span: The proposed rule defines covered semiconductor products or services to include those designed, produced, or provided by SMIC, and by CXMT or YMTC.

event_title: Proposed FAR rule adds rapid reporting and non-federal disclosure duties
event_summary: The proposed rule pairs the prohibition with 72-hour government reporting for suspected covered semiconductors after December 23, 2027 and requires certain semiconductor covered entities to disclose covered content to non-federal customers.
actor: FAR Council / OFPP / DoD / GSA / NASA
target_entity: Federal contractors and semiconductor covered entities
direct_impact: Converts the covered-semiconductor issue into an ongoing reporting, disclosure, and remediation obligation for contractors.
indirect_impact: Raises downstream compliance and corrective-action exposure even when a product reaches the market outside direct federal sales.
evidence_span: The proposed rule requires 72-hour reporting after December 23, 2027 for covered semiconductor discoveries and disclosure to non-federal customers by semiconductor covered entities.

event_title: AI Diffusion rule creates worldwide licensing framework for advanced-computing IC clusters
event_summary: BIS issued the AI Diffusion framework, imposing a worldwide regional stability license requirement for advanced-computing ICs and building authorization pathways for secure ecosystems and approved end users.
target_entity: Advanced-computing IC clusters and AI-capable end users
direct_impact: Extends the control logic from destination-specific restrictions toward a global governance framework for frontier AI compute diffusion.
indirect_impact: Changes risk from simple destination blocking to tiered ecosystem governance and compliance screening for large compute deployments.
evidence_span: The AI Diffusion IFR imposed a worldwide RS license requirement for advanced-computing ICs and created exceptions and pathways to authorization for low-risk transactions.

event_title: BIS adds foundry and OSAT due-diligence measures for advanced-computing ICs
event_summary: BIS concluded that existing Entity List entries, Red Flag guidance, and technical guidance were not enough for foundries and OSATs to definitively determine whether chips met 3A090 thresholds, so it added due-diligence procedures and clarifications.
target_entity: Foundries and OSATs in advanced IC supply chains
direct_impact: Turns technical export-control rules into operational screening obligations for manufacturing and packaging actors in the chip supply chain.
indirect_impact: Raises transaction-screening, customer-identification, and recordkeeping burdens for foundries and OSATs handling advanced IC designs.
evidence_span: BIS assessed that existing Entity List entries, Red Flag requirements and technical guidance had not fully ensured that foundries could definitively determine whether ICs met ECCN 3A090 parameters.

event_title: Foundry Entity List action adds 16 entities tied to advanced IC support and Huawei diversion risk
event_summary: BIS added 16 entities in China and Singapore to the Entity List, including Sophgo-linked organizations, citing support for advanced computing IC development and risk of diversion to Huawei.
target_entity: Sophgo-linked and associated foundry ecosystem entities
direct_impact: Moves from general technical tightening to targeted enforcement against entities tied to advanced IC production and diversion risk.
indirect_impact: Signals that foundry-side and design-side circumvention risks can trigger direct Entity List action, not just broader technical controls.
evidence_span: BIS added 16 entities because they supported advanced computing IC development and posed a risk of diversion to Huawei, with footnote 4 designations for their role.

event_title: BIS corrects ECCN 3A090 license requirement after January 2025 due-diligence IFR
event_summary: BIS issued a correcting amendment to revise the ECCN 3A090 license requirement table, clarifying that regional stability controls apply to 3A090.a and that worldwide license requirements attach to certain 3A090.a items.
direct_impact: Prevents misinterpretation of the January 2025 rule and stabilizes the legal basis for compliance treatment of 3A090.a items.
indirect_impact: Reduces ambiguity but also confirms that exporters must treat certain 3A090.a items as subject to worldwide licensing requirements.
evidence_span: The correction revised ECCN 3A090 to state that RS applies to 3A090.a and explained the worldwide license requirement for certain 3A090.a items.

event_title: BIS shifts some commercially available advanced chips to case-by-case review for China and Macau
event_summary: BIS revised the license review policy for certain semiconductors such as Nvidia H200-class products and equivalents from presumption of denial to case-by-case review, subject to supply, security, and testing conditions.
target_entity: Certain commercially available advanced-computing commodities to China and Macau
direct_impact: Introduces a calibrated licensing channel without abandoning the broader advanced-computing control regime.
indirect_impact: Adds a more conditional and evidence-heavy path for exports, making compliance hinge on capacity, security, and third-party verification claims.
evidence_span: BIS changed the policy from presumption of denial to case-by-case review for certain commercially available semiconductors to China and Macau under specified conditions.

event_title: AI Diffusion framework reinforces the policy significance of HBM-linked memory constraints
event_summary: The AI Diffusion rule formalized a worldwide licensing framework for advanced-computing IC clusters and explicitly situated HBM as a critical component of advanced AI training and inference, reinforcing the strategic significance of memory-linked controls.
target_entity: Advanced-computing IC clusters and HBM-linked ecosystems
direct_impact: Reframes HBM and memory-adjacent controls as part of a broader global compute-governance regime rather than a narrow December 2024 refinement.
indirect_impact: Deepens strategic risk for memory-fab actors by tying memory capacity and bandwidth more tightly to AI diffusion governance.
evidence_span: The AI Diffusion rule explained that effective December 2, 2024 BIS had imposed new controls on HBM commodities critical to AI training and inference at scale.

event_title: Proposed FAR rule adds reporting and disclosure duties for covered semiconductor discoveries involving named sources
event_summary: The February 2026 proposed FAR rule pairs the procurement prohibition with 72-hour government reporting and non-federal disclosure duties, increasing downstream compliance exposure once covered sources such as YMTC are named.
actor: FAR Council / OFPP / DoD / GSA / NASA
target_entity: Federal contractors and semiconductor covered entities
direct_impact: Extends YMTC-related risk from sourcing prohibition into ongoing reporting, disclosure, and corrective-action obligations.
indirect_impact: Creates secondary compliance exposure for contractors that may discover YMTC-linked content in their supply chains after procurement decisions are made.
evidence_span: The proposed rule requires 72-hour reporting for covered semiconductor discoveries and disclosure to non-federal customers by semiconductor covered entities.

event_title: December 2024 BIS IFR creates new advanced-node, HBM, and Footnote 5 FDP controls
event_summary: BIS refined advanced-computing and semiconductor-manufacturing controls by adding HBM controls, creating new FDP rules to impair advanced-node IC production by destinations or entities of concern, and adding Footnote 5 Entity List FDP provisions.
direct_impact: Establishes the technical and jurisdictional base for later foundry-focused compliance and Entity List actions.
indirect_impact: Raises risk for offshore foundries and advanced-node supply chains that depend on U.S.-origin software, technology, or tools.
evidence_span: The December 2024 IFR added HBM controls, created new FDP rules to impair advanced-node IC production, and introduced Footnote 5 Entity List FDP provisions.

event_title: AI Diffusion rule globalizes frontier-compute licensing logic
event_summary: BIS issued the AI Diffusion framework to govern the staged, responsible diffusion of advanced AI by controlling exports of large quantities of advanced-computing ICs through a worldwide licensing and authorization structure.
direct_impact: Moves the policy problem from bilateral export denial toward global compute-governance and secure-ecosystem management.
indirect_impact: Raises compliance complexity for firms routing advanced compute across multiple jurisdictions, even outside the PRC-focused context.
evidence_span: The rule imposed a worldwide RS license requirement and sought to allow large quantities of advanced-computing ICs only to certain destinations and end users that meet security and safety standards.

event_title: January 2025 IFR adds foundry and OSAT due-diligence procedures
event_summary: BIS added due-diligence measures because existing guidance had not fully ensured that foundries and related supply-chain actors could determine whether chips met advanced-computing control thresholds, especially around applicable advanced logic ICs.
target_entity: Foundries, OSATs, and IC supply-chain actors
direct_impact: Transforms technical uncertainty into a compliance duty for manufacturers, packagers, and related supply-chain participants.
indirect_impact: Increases screening and diligence burdens for foundries serving fabless designers and intermediaries with possible diversion exposure.
evidence_span: BIS stated that foundries producing ICs for IC designers and other related companies had not been able to definitively determine whether those ICs met or exceeded ECCN 3A090 control parameters.

event_title: Foundry Entity List action targets Sophgo-linked entities over advanced-IC support and Huawei diversion risk
event_summary: BIS added 16 entities, including multiple Sophgo-linked organizations, because they supported advanced computing IC development and posed diversion risk to Huawei, showing direct enforcement against the foundry/design ecosystem.
direct_impact: Turns a generalized concern about foundry-side circumvention into direct end-user restrictions aimed at specific actors and diversion pathways.
indirect_impact: Signals that advanced-IC design and foundry ecosystems can be targeted when they intersect with Huawei-related diversion or military-surveillance concerns.
evidence_span: BIS added 16 entities due to support for advanced computing IC development and because they posed a risk of diversion to Huawei.

event_title: February 2025 correction clarifies the 3A090.a license requirement after the foundry IFR
event_summary: BIS issued a correction to the January 16 due-diligence IFR, revising the ECCN 3A090 table to ensure exporters and supply-chain actors applied the intended worldwide license requirement logic to 3A090.a items.
target_entity: Exporters and supply-chain actors handling ECCN 3A090.a items
direct_impact: Shows that even small wording errors in advanced-computing controls can materially affect downstream compliance implementation.
indirect_impact: Reinforces that foundry-facing diligence depends on precise treatment of thresholded 3A090.a items and worldwide license coverage.
evidence_span: The correction revised ECCN 3A090 so that RS applies to 3A090.a and clarified the worldwide license requirement for certain 3A090.a items.

event_title: January 2026 rule recalibrates review policy for certain commercially available advanced chips
event_summary: BIS moved certain commercially available advanced chips such as Nvidia H200-class products from presumption of denial to case-by-case review for China and Macau, but only with strict conditions on supply, foundry-capacity diversion, security procedures, and third-party testing.
target_entity: Certain commercially available advanced chips to China and Macau
direct_impact: Represents a conditional recalibration rather than a repeal of the earlier foundry and diffusion control logic.
indirect_impact: Shows that later policy may reopen narrow export channels while still embedding foundry-capacity and security concerns into licensing review.
evidence_span: The 2026 rule allowed case-by-case review only if exporters certify sufficient U.S. supply, no diversion of global foundry capacity, strong security procedures, and independent third-party testing.

event_title: 2024 FAR ANPR opens the procurement-rulemaking pathway later used to capture named semiconductor sources
event_summary: The 2024 advance notice launched implementation of Section 5949, creating the policy pathway that would later be used to define and prohibit covered semiconductor products and services from named sources such as CXMT.
direct_impact: Federal procurement moved from a statutory idea to an operational rulemaking track that could later be attached to named Chinese semiconductor sources.
indirect_impact: The rulemaking pathway increased the probability that future named-source restrictions would extend beyond export controls into federal acquisition and contractor compliance.
evidence_span: Section 5949 implementation planning created the architecture for later procurement restrictions on covered semiconductors.

event_title: December 2024 BIS rule imposes HBM controls, elevating advanced memory in frontier-AI controls
event_summary: BIS imposed new controls on certain high-bandwidth memory commodities, signaling that advanced memory capacity and bandwidth had become a strategic choke point for frontier AI and supercomputing applications.
target_entity: HBM commodities / PRC memory-linked supply chain
direct_impact: Advanced memory moved closer to the center of U.S. control policy for frontier AI capability.
indirect_impact: Memory producers and memory-linked supply chains faced stronger strategic and compliance scrutiny as HBM became part of the advanced-computing control stack.
evidence_span: The rule adds new controls on certain high-bandwidth memory commodities needed for advanced AI models and supercomputing applications.

event_title: DoD Chinese military companies notice includes CXMT
event_summary: The Department of Defense published a notice making available its Chinese military companies designation list, which includes ChangXin Memory Technologies, Inc. (CXMT), adding a formal national-security screening signal around the company.
direct_impact: CXMT became subject to a formal U.S. government military-affiliation screening signal outside BIS export rules.
indirect_impact: The designation can intensify downstream diligence, procurement screening, and policy justification for later restrictions affecting federal acquisition or supply-chain review.
evidence_span: The published Chinese military companies notice makes available a list that includes ChangXin Memory Technologies, Inc. (CXMT).

event_title: AI Diffusion framework reinforces the strategic role of HBM in advanced AI clusters
event_summary: The AI Diffusion framework placed advanced-computing IC clusters under a worldwide licensing architecture and reinforced the centrality of memory bandwidth and cluster integrity in frontier AI capability, amplifying the strategic meaning of HBM-related controls.
target_entity: Advanced-computing IC clusters and HBM-linked supply chain
direct_impact: HBM-related memory restrictions became more tightly integrated into the logic of frontier-AI governance.
indirect_impact: Memory producers linked to advanced AI clusters faced a broader licensing and diffusion-control context rather than a narrow item-specific control environment.
evidence_span: The framework imposed worldwide licensing logic for advanced-computing IC clusters and authorization pathways for low-diversion transactions.

event_title: Proposed FAR rule explicitly names CXMT as a covered semiconductor source
event_summary: The February 2026 proposed FAR rule operationalizes the covered-semiconductor prohibition by expressly naming CXMT, along with other PRC firms, in the covered-source definition used for federal procurement compliance.
direct_impact: CXMT moved from a screened memory actor to an explicitly named source in the federal procurement prohibition framework.
indirect_impact: The naming sharpens downstream exclusion risk for products and services touching federal contracting channels.
evidence_span: The proposed rule defines covered semiconductor products or services to include those designed, produced, or provided by SMIC, CXMT, or YMTC and their subsidiaries, affiliates, or successors.

event_title: Proposed FAR rule adds CXMT-linked reporting and disclosure obligations
event_summary: The February 2026 proposed FAR rule pairs the naming of covered semiconductor sources with 72-hour government reporting and certain non-federal disclosure duties, increasing traceability and remediation obligations when covered semiconductors are discovered.
direct_impact: Compliance obligations expand beyond sourcing prohibition into mandatory reporting and disclosure workflows.
indirect_impact: The risk shifts from simple exclusion to operational compliance burden, remediation, and customer-notification exposure.
evidence_span: The proposed rule adds 72-hour government reporting and certain non-federal disclosure obligations when covered semiconductors are discovered.

event_title: DoD notice naming CXMT adds a federal security-screening signal ahead of procurement implementation
event_summary: By formally publishing a Chinese military companies notice that included CXMT, DoD added another federal security-screening signal relevant to how future covered-semiconductor procurement restrictions might be understood and justified.
direct_impact: Federal screening pressure around CXMT broadened beyond BIS export control logic.
indirect_impact: This signal can strengthen later procurement-side diligence and justification for named-source restrictions.
evidence_span: The published Chinese military companies notice includes CXMT, adding a federal security-screening signal outside EAR administration.

event_title: Proposed FAR rule phases in exceptions for existing equipment and no-alternative-source scenarios
event_summary: The proposed FAR rule does not require immediate removal or replacement of covered semiconductor products already resident in equipment before December 23, 2027 and provides a temporary exception for commercial products or services where no alternative sources are available until December 23, 2028.
direct_impact: The prohibition is operationalized with transition and availability exceptions rather than instant blanket removal.
indirect_impact: The phased exceptions soften immediate disruption but preserve long-run exclusion and compliance obligations.
evidence_span: The proposed rule does not require remove-or-replace before December 23, 2027 and excepts some no-alternative-source commercial products until December 23, 2028.

event_title: BIS adds GMC Semiconductor and Jicun for acquiring U.S.-origin semiconductor manufacturing equipment for SMIC-listed fabs
event_summary: BIS added GMC Semiconductor Technology (Wuxi) and Jicun Semiconductor Technology (Shanghai) to the Entity List after concluding that they acquired U.S.-origin semiconductor manufacturing equipment for SMIC Northern and Semiconductor Manufacturing International (Beijing) Corporation without required authorization.
target_entity: GMC Semiconductor Technology (Wuxi) and Jicun Semiconductor Technology (Shanghai)
third_party_entity: SMIC Northern / Semiconductor Manufacturing International (Beijing) Corporation
direct_impact: SMIC-related controls extended into downstream diversion-enforcement against intermediaries that procured equipment for listed fabs.
indirect_impact: The action demonstrates that firm-specific and sectoral semiconductor controls can later be enforced through anti-diversion targeting of facilitators and procurement channels.
evidence_span: These entities acquired U.S.-origin semiconductor manufacturing equipment for two Entity List parties, SMIC Northern and Semiconductor Manufacturing International (Beijing), without requisite BIS authorization.

event_title: BIS adds NetForward entities for circumvention and diversion risk to entity-listed parties
event_summary: BIS added several NetForward entities because they posed a risk of circumvention of export controls and diversion to entity-listed parties, showing a broader anti-evasion turn in semiconductor-related enforcement.
direct_impact: Enforcement moved beyond direct suppliers to firms associated with circumvention or diversion risk around listed parties.
indirect_impact: The action broadens compliance risk across semiconductor-adjacent firms even where they are not the primary listed manufacturer.
evidence_span: The NetForward entities were added because they pose a risk of circumvention of export controls and diversion to entity-listed parties.

event_title: September 2025 Entity List action shows continued anti-diversion enforcement within the semiconductor ecosystem
event_summary: The September 2025 BIS rule added diversion- and circumvention-linked entities, demonstrating that the advanced-computing control regime continued to generate follow-on entity-list enforcement inside the semiconductor ecosystem after the January 2025 rule wave.
direct_impact: The advanced-controls regime remained active through follow-on enforcement against ecosystem intermediaries.
indirect_impact: Later enforcement supports the interpretation that technical rules alone did not end the need for actor-specific anti-diversion action.
evidence_span: BIS added entities linked to diversion and circumvention risk in the semiconductor ecosystem, including procurement for SMIC-listed parties and risk of diversion to listed parties.

event_title: BIS adds GMC and Jicun for supplying SME to SMIC-listed parties without authorization
event_summary: The September 2025 BIS action added GMC and Jicun because they acquired U.S.-origin semiconductor manufacturing equipment for SMIC-listed fabs, extending the anti-diversion story from the January 2025 foundry-focused actions into later procurement enforcement.
target_entity: GMC Semiconductor Technology (Wuxi) and Jicun Semiconductor Technology (Shanghai)
third_party_entity: SMIC Northern / Semiconductor Manufacturing International (Beijing) Corporation
direct_impact: Diversion-enforcement logic migrated from Huawei-linked/Sophgo-linked concerns to equipment procurement for SMIC-listed fabs.
indirect_impact: Foundry and intermediary firms face heightened diligence risk even after earlier rule clarifications and entity actions.
evidence_span: BIS added GMC and Jicun for acquiring U.S.-origin SME for SMIC-listed fabs without required authorization.

event_title: BIS adds NetForward entities for circumvention and diversion to listed parties
event_summary: The same September 2025 rule added NetForward entities because they posed a risk of circumvention of export controls and diversion to entity-listed parties, broadening the enforcement lens from named foundry-linked actors to wider evasion networks.
direct_impact: Foundry-related compliance risk broadened into a more general anti-circumvention enforcement posture.
indirect_impact: This expands the compliance story from specific named design/foundry actors to broader front-company or circumvention-network risk.
evidence_span: BIS added the NetForward entities because they pose a risk of circumvention of export controls and diversion to entity-listed parties.

event_title: Data Center VEU framework expands trusted export pathway for approved data-center end users
event_summary: BIS expanded the VEU program to include Data Center VEU, creating a preapproved pathway for exports and reexports of certain controlled items needed for approved data centers and advanced-computing deployments.
direct_impact: Created a facilitative channel for selected trusted end users to receive certain controlled items with reduced transactional friction.
indirect_impact: Established a contrast point for later tightening around trusted fabs and data-center supply chains.
evidence_span: This expansion of the VEU program to include Data Center VEU is intended to facilitate quick and reliable export or reexport of items on the Commerce Control List necessary for a data center to preapproved trusted end users.

event_title: AI Diffusion rule differentiates access to frontier compute infrastructure worldwide
event_summary: The AI Diffusion rule introduced a global licensing framework for advanced-computing ICs and large compute clusters, sharpening how trusted and restricted destinations are treated in frontier-compute supply chains.
direct_impact: Raised the policy stakes for who may obtain frontier compute at scale and under what licensing conditions.
indirect_impact: Made trusted-status questions more consequential for fabs, cloud operators, and advanced-node supply chains.
evidence_span: The rule imposed additional export controls on certain advanced computing ICs, computer commodities that contain such ICs, and certain model weights, while creating differentiated authorization paths.

event_title: Foundry and OSAT due-diligence rule adds compliance duties inside the advanced-IC supply chain
event_summary: BIS required additional due-diligence procedures for foundries and OSATs handling advanced-computing ICs, adding supply-chain compliance checks around high-risk advanced-node manufacturing and assembly flows.
direct_impact: Imposed affirmative screening and recordkeeping burdens on supply-chain intermediaries handling advanced-computing chips.
indirect_impact: Prepared the ground for later anti-diversion and trust-erosion actions involving PRC fabs and associated ecosystems.
evidence_span: This interim final rule will protect the national security of the United States and assist foundries and OSATs in complying with provisions of the EAR pertaining to advanced computing ICs in the supply chain.

event_title: BIS revokes PRC VEU authorizations for Intel Dalian, Samsung China, and SK hynix China
event_summary: The End-User Review Committee removed Intel Semiconductor (Dalian), Samsung China Semiconductor, and SK hynix Semiconductor (China) from the VEU program, signaling a sharper trust erosion for PRC fabs previously treated as preapproved end users.
target_entity: Intel Semiconductor (Dalian); Samsung China Semiconductor; SK hynix Semiconductor (China)
direct_impact: Eliminated VEU-based facilitation for three major PRC fabs, raising compliance friction and uncertainty for future exports.
indirect_impact: Marked a turning point from selective trust facilitation toward explicit revocation of preapproved status in China-based semiconductor manufacturing.
evidence_span: The ERC determined to remove Intel Semiconductor (Dalian) Ltd, Samsung China Semiconductor Co. Ltd, and SK hynix Semiconductor (China) Ltd from the Validated End User Program.

event_title: BIS recalibrates review policy for some commercially available advanced chips
event_summary: BIS revised its license review policy for certain advanced-computing commodities to China and Macau from a presumption of denial to case-by-case review, creating a narrower recalibration at the chip-review layer without restoring earlier trusted-fab authorizations.
direct_impact: Some commercially available advanced chips moved to a case-by-case review posture.
indirect_impact: Did not by itself restore VEU trust or eliminate foundry due-diligence obligations for China-linked fabs.
evidence_span: The Bureau of Industry and Security is revising its license review policy for exports of certain semiconductors to China and Macau—changing it from a presumption of denial to a case-by-case review.

event_title: January 2025 Entity List action adds 16 entities tied to advanced-IC support risks
event_summary: BIS added 16 entities to the Entity List, with most entries under China and some under Singapore, expanding January 2025 pressure on networks linked to advanced-IC support and other restricted activities.
target_entity: 16 newly listed entities under China and Singapore
direct_impact: Immediately subjected listed entities to EAR-based license requirements, generally under a presumption of denial.
indirect_impact: Opened a year of repeated Entity List expansion and anti-diversion enforcement around semiconductor and advanced-IC ecosystems.
evidence_span: BIS amends the EAR by adding 16 entities to the Entity List, under the destinations of China (14) and Singapore (2).

event_title: Concurrent January 2025 rule revises an Entity List entry and adds more PRC-linked entities
event_summary: On the same day, BIS issued a separate Entity List action adding entities and revising an existing entry tied to PRC military modernization and advanced-technology development concerns.
target_entity: PRC-linked entities and one revised Entity List entry
direct_impact: Expanded January 2025 Entity List pressure beyond the 16-entity rule alone.
indirect_impact: Signaled that 2025 enforcement would combine both new listings and revisions of existing entries.
evidence_span: These additions are being made because these entities advance the People’s Republic of China’s military modernization through the development and integration of advanced artificial intelligence research.

event_title: March 2025 BIS rule adds 70 entities to the Entity List
event_summary: BIS broadened the Entity List in March 2025 by adding 70 entities and modifying existing entries, representing the year’s largest single expansion in this chain.
direct_impact: Substantially widened the enforcement perimeter around targeted networks and supply chains.
indirect_impact: Created the broadest 2025 Entity List escalation in this dataset and set up later anti-diversion targeting.
evidence_span: In this final rule, the Bureau of Industry and Security amends the EAR by adding 70 entities to the Entity List.

event_title: September 2025 anti-diversion Entity List action targets supply to SMIC-listed fabs and other restricted parties
event_summary: BIS added and revised entries in September 2025, including entities that acquired U.S.-origin semiconductor-manufacturing equipment for SMIC-listed fabs, making the anti-diversion logic explicit.
target_entity: GMC Semiconductor; Jicun Semiconductor; other listed parties
direct_impact: Tightened restrictions on firms accused of acquiring or diverting U.S.-origin items for already listed semiconductor parties.
indirect_impact: Shifted the chain from broad perimeter expansion toward explicit diversion-enforcement in semiconductor manufacturing networks.
evidence_span: These additions are being made because these entities acquired U.S.-origin semiconductor manufacturing equipment for two Entity List parties, SMIC Northern Integrated Circuit Manufacturing (Beijing) Co., Ltd.

event_title: October 2025 rule adds 29 entries and widens the geographic circumvention perimeter
event_summary: BIS added 29 entries—26 entities and three addresses—under China, Turkey, and the UAE, showing that Entity List enforcement had widened from PRC-centered additions to a broader anti-circumvention perimeter.
target_entity: 29 entries under China, Turkey, and UAE
direct_impact: Extended Entity List exposure beyond China-centered actors to third-country routing and procurement networks.
indirect_impact: Indicated that 2025 enforcement pressure had become both geographically broader and more circumvention-focused.
evidence_span: BIS amends the EAR by adding 29 entries (26 entities and 3 addresses) to the Entity List under the destinations of China (19), Turkey (9), and the UAE (1).

event_title: EO 14141 prioritizes building frontier AI infrastructure in the United States
event_summary: Executive Order 14141 framed domestic frontier-AI buildout as a strategic objective, reinforcing the security logic behind expanding domestic AI and semiconductor capacity.
target_entity: U.S. AI infrastructure and semiconductor ecosystem
direct_impact: Signaled a policy preference for building future frontier AI infrastructure in the United States.
indirect_impact: Provided an industrial-policy backdrop for later security measures on semiconductor imports and procurement.
evidence_span: This order sets our Nation on the path to ensure that future frontier AI can, and will, continue to be built here in the United States.

event_title: Section 232 proclamation finds semiconductor and SME imports threaten U.S. national security
event_summary: A January 2026 proclamation concurred with the Secretary of Commerce that semiconductors, semiconductor-manufacturing equipment, and their derivative products were being imported under circumstances that threaten to impair national security.
target_entity: Imports of semiconductors, SME, and derivative products
direct_impact: Converted industrial-security concerns into a formal national-security finding under section 232.
indirect_impact: Created a legal and policy bridge from domestic-capacity concerns to import-adjustment measures.
evidence_span: I concur with the Secretary’s finding that semiconductors, semiconductor manufacturing equipment, and their derivative products are being imported into the United States in such quantities and under such circumstances as to threaten to impair the national security of the United States.

event_title: Section 232 proclamation directs a negotiation-based plan of action to adjust semiconductor imports
event_summary: The same proclamation directed the Secretary of Commerce and USTR to pursue negotiations and other actions to adjust imports so they would not threaten national security.
direct_impact: Moved from diagnosis to planned intervention by ordering a negotiation-based import-adjustment process.
indirect_impact: Showed that semiconductor security policy was expanding beyond export controls into import management and negotiation.
evidence_span: I have also determined that it is necessary and appropriate to adopt a plan of action ... to adjust such imports of semiconductors, semiconductor manufacturing equipment, and their derivative products so that such imports will not threaten to impair the national security of the United States.

event_title: FAR proposed rule translates semiconductor security concerns into procurement prohibition architecture
event_summary: The February 2026 FAR proposed rule moved semiconductor security from export/import controls into procurement law by proposing broad prohibitions on certain covered semiconductor products and services.
direct_impact: Projected security restrictions into federal procurement, potentially constraining vendors and integrators using covered sources.
indirect_impact: Connected industrial-security logic to downstream government purchasing and contractor compliance.
evidence_span: This proposed rule requires that prime contractors insert FAR 52.240-YY into all subcontracts for the supply of any electronic products and services.

event_title: FAR proposal imposes subcontract flow-down and rapid reporting/disclosure duties
event_summary: The February 2026 proposal required contractors to flow down restrictions to subcontractors and added rapid reporting and non-federal disclosure duties when covered semiconductor content is discovered.
direct_impact: Raised the compliance burden beyond direct procurement to subcontracting chains and post-discovery reporting.
indirect_impact: Turned semiconductor-security screening into a distributed contractor compliance task.
evidence_span: This proposed rule requires that prime contractors insert FAR 52.240-YY into all subcontracts for the supply of any electronic products and services.
